title: Elderberry and ginger decoction for immune support
subtitle: A warming antiviral decoction for the early days of a cold
excerpt: Dried elderberries and fresh ginger simmered in water to make a concentrated decoction. Taken during the first days of a cold or flu. Elderberries must be cooked, raw elderberries are mildly toxic. The combination is one of the most evidence-supported traditional immune preparations.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), elder entry, elderberry wine and decoction for colds, flu, and fever. WHO Monograph on Sambucus nigra (1999), antiviral and immune-stimulating properties of elderberry extracts. Zakay-Rones et al. (2004), 'Randomized study of the efficacy and safety of oral elderberry extract in the treatment of influenza A and B virus infections', limited clinical evidence for reduced flu duration.

content.text: Dried elderberries and fresh ginger simmered together in water for 30 minutes, strained, and sipped hot. This is a

rather than an infusion. The berries are too tough and dense for a simple steep. They must be simmered to break down the cyanogenic glycosides that make raw elderberries mildly toxic. Old herbals record elderberry brews and wines as winter cold and flu remedies. Modern lab work and clinical trials back the antiviral action and the shorter flu episodes. The ginger adds warmth, calms a queasy stomach, and gives a mild blood-thinning effect that rounds out the brew.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Raw elderberries are toxic: raw or undercooked elderberries cause nausea and vomiting. Always simmer for at least 20 minutes. Do not use fresh garden elderberries without thorough cooking. Dried elderberries from a herbal supplier are safe in this drink after simmering.

Pregnancy: small elderberry amounts in cooking are widely considered safe. Strong daily brews in pregnancy are less studied. Talk to a midwife first.

Blood thinning: ginger with elderberry has a mild blood-thinning effect. See the ginger safety note for details.

See your doctor for flu with a high fever for more than three days, breathlessness, chest pain, or a sudden worsening after early improvement. Do not delay medical care for serious flu by relying on herbal drinks alone.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Dried elderberries
items.prepNote: dried Sambucus nigra berries from a herbal supplier

items.name: Ginger
items.prepNote: fresh, sliced into thin rounds, about a 3 cm piece

items.name: Honey
items.prepNote: optional, stir in once the decoction has cooled below 60°C

content.text: Place {{dried-elderberries}} of dried elderberries and {{ginger-root}} of sliced fresh ginger in the small saucepan. Add {{water}} of cold water. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, then reduce the heat and simmer covered for 30 minutes. The liquid will reduce and turn very dark purple-red. Strain through a sieve, pressing the berries firmly with a spoon to draw out all the liquid. Allow to cool slightly before stirring in honey if using. Drink 100 ml of the warm brew as a dose; keep the rest in the fridge.

Adults: 100 ml three times daily at the onset of cold or flu symptoms, taken warm. Continue for five to seven days. The traditional course for elderberry preparations is short and targeted, at the early, acute phase of infection. Do not use as a substitute for rest, fluids, and appropriate medical care if symptoms are severe.

Keeps in the fridge in a sealed glass jar for 5 days. Reheat gently in a small saucepan before each dose. Do not microwave. It heats the brew unevenly and can create hot spots if honey has been added.
When NOT to use this remedy

Never use raw or undercooked elderberries. See your doctor for a high fever lasting more than three days, breathlessness, chest pain, confusion, or rapid worsening. Do not delay medical care for a flu that is getting worse. Antiviral medicines work best when started early.
